nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, this species forms aggregations around isolated coral heads and rubble patches in reef environments, typically at depths of 10–58 m, though it has been recorded from as shallow as 3 m to as deep as 152 m. Juveniles are also found in harbors and silty rocky reefs.

diet.notes: In the wild, primarily feeds on zooplankton drifting around aggregations, with opportunistic predation on very small fish. In captivity, generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, copepods, and high-quality pellet/flake formulations. Multiple small feedings per day (3–5 times) are commonly advised; like most anthias, individuals that go off food can decline rapidly. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods is helpful for supplemental live prey.

avoidWith: fish that compete aggressively for food

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Izuzuki, 640x480. Sharp, vibrant male showing the diagnostic bold vertical red band on a pink/white body with orange head and elongated fins; whole fish in frame against dark reef.
Photo by Izuzuki, 640x426. Second sharp male shot clearly showing the red vertical band and orange head; near-duplicate scene to the recommended pick but slightly different crop.
Photo by Izuzuki, 640x480. Sharp, vibrant female form (orange/pink body, blue-edged fins, red caudal tips) with no red band; adds variety showing the female that dominates the harem.

summary: A colorful Indo-Pacific anthias identified by a bold vertical red band crossing the body against a background of warm pink and orange. Generally considered reef-safe, it is typically kept in a haremic group with one dominant male and several females.
careNotes:
The Red Belted Anthias (Pseudanthias rubrizonatus) is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning all individuals begin life as female, and the dominant female in a group will transition to male when the existing male is removed. In the wild, they form aggregations around isolated coral heads and rubble patches, organized into harems of one dominant male and multiple females. Aquarists typically replicate this structure by keeping a single male with four or more females. Two males kept together tend to fight intensely.

This species tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics and other anthias species competing for the same niche. It is generally considered reef-safe, with no documented harm to corals or sessile invertebrates.

Like other anthias, individuals are constant-grazing planktivores in the wild and benefit from three to five small feedings per day in captivity. An established refugium producing copepods and amphipods is highly beneficial as a supplemental live-food source. A tightly sealed aquarium lid is strongly advised — this species is considered a high escape risk.

In the wild, the Red Belted Anthias is found across the Indo-Pacific from the Andaman Sea east to Fiji and the Solomon Islands, north to southern Japan, and south to northwestern Australia and the Great Barrier Reef. Typical depth is 10–58 m, though it has been recorded as deep as 152 m. Juveniles are often found in harbors and silty rocky reefs.
breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. As a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, sex change from the dominant female to male can occur in aquaria when the male is removed. No established captive-breeding protocol is documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: water parameters, tank-size minimum, temperament, diet, reef-safe status, color description, and harem grouping.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Randall, 1983), taxonomy (Anthiadidae, Perciformes), maximum length (12 cm TL), depth range (10–133 m; typically 10–58 m), and Indo-Pacific distribution. Listed preferred temperature 24.2–28.9°C — averaged with LiveAquaria's 22–26°C to give 23–27°C in the entry.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Tertiary check (taxonomy fallback only): confirmed family Anthiadidae, depth range 3–152 m, protogynous sex change, and harem social structure (one dominant male, multiple females, dominant female transitions to male if male disappears).

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. Protogynous sequential hermaphrodite; dominant female transitions to male when the group's male is absent. No established captive-breeding protocol documented.
